one_line_takeaway: An engineered chain-of-thought prompt plus Function Calling turns GPT-4 into a 99.70%-accurate, explanation-producing phishing-email detector that beats trained ML baselines by analyzing both headers and body — but at real per-email API cost and with an untested attack surface.
key_finding: GPT-4 with the engineered normal prompt achieved 99.70% accuracy, precision, and recall (TP=1007, FP=3, TN=997, FN=3), outperforming all other LLMs and all baselines. The engineered prompt improved recall for every model over the simple prompt (the simple prompt biased models toward the 'legitimate' class), most dramatically boosting GPT-3.5 recall by 28.02% and Llama2 by 15.84% — though for Llama2 the normal prompt raised false positives and cut precision by 26.41%. GPT-4's phishing_score was well-calibrated (legitimate emails scored 0-4, most TN at 0; phishing scored 6-10, peaking at 8-9). Qualitatively, GPT-4 leveraged pre-trained brand-to-domain knowledge to flag impersonation even when SPF/DKIM passed, decoded homoglyph/Unicode evasion, and detected 124 phishing emails that impersonated no brand purely via SE cues.
core_contribution: ChatSpamDetector is a system that repurposes general-purpose LLMs (GPT-4, GPT-3.5, Llama2-70B, Gemini Pro) as phishing-email detectors. Its own novel contributions are: (1) a pipeline that parses .eml files, decodes headers/bodies, strips authentication and X- headers for experimental fairness, and simplifies long/HTML emails to fit a 3,000-token budget while preserving phishing-relevant signals (a-tags, deceptive text); (2) an engineered 5-subtask, chain-of-thought 'normal' prompt with a spam-detector persona and explicit examples of social-engineering (SE) techniques; (3) use of OpenAI Function Calling to force structured JSON output (is_phishing, phishing_score 0-10, brand_impersonated, rationales up to 500 words, brief_reason) so the system yields both a verdict AND a human-readable rationale. The authors position this as the first study to systematically evaluate LLMs' ability to distinguish phishing from legitimate emails using a dataset containing BOTH classes (distinguishing themselves from cited work like Heiding et al. [22], which focused on generating phishing and detecting malicious intent, and body-only detectors like Chataut et al. [12] and Si et al. [46]).
threat_model: Defensive detection tool. Adversary = phishing email senders who impersonate brands, spoof display names/sender domains, embed deceptive HTML hyperlinks (visible text differs from href), and use evasion tricks to bypass spam filters and email authentication (SPF/DKIM/DMARC). Documented evasion tactics the system must catch include homoglyph/Unicode substitution (Cyrillic 'p' in 'aypal', alpha for 'a' in 'PayPal', U+070F Syriac Abbreviation Mark separators), URL shorteners, look-alike domains, and abuse of legitimate services (Google Apps Script, Google Forms, Bing redirects) to host scam content. Scope is explicitly limited to phishing emails that contain a URL/link to a phishing site; malware attachments and payment-request-only scams are out of scope. The paper does not deeply treat adversarial attacks against the LLM detector itself (e.g., prompt injection embedded in email bodies), which is a notable gap.
limitations: (1) Scope excludes phishing without body URLs — malware-attachment and pure payment-request scams are not evaluated. (2) Only default LLM parameters tested; temperature/top_p tuning unexplored. (3) No RAG, so the system cannot recognize brands or domains that emerged after the model's training cutoff; the two FTX false negatives and a Tether Cashback FN slipped through because authentication passed and links used legitimate domains. (4) Cost at scale: analyzing all 2,010 emails cost $265.80 on GPT-4 (vs $13.68 GPT-3.5), making org-wide deployment expensive. (5) Legitimate emails came from the older CSDMC corpus while phishing came from a recent 2022-2023 honeypot source — a temporal/source mismatch between classes that could inflate separability. (6) No evaluation of prompt-injection resistance, i.e., an attacker crafting email text to manipulate the LLM judge. (7) Baselines were trained on out-of-distribution datasets, so their low scores partly reflect domain shift rather than pure inferiority.

datasets_used: phishing_pot honeypot repository [7]: 1,010 phishing emails collected Aug 2022-Oct 2023, all containing body URLs, spanning 19 languages (627 English, 128 Portuguese, 95 German, 79 Dutch) and impersonating 193 brands
CSDMC SPAM corpus [2]: 1,000 legitimate 'ham' emails randomly sampled from 2,949 (12 languages: 895 English, 33 Dutch, 30 French, 25 Romanian)
Combined evaluation set: 2,010 emails (1,010 phishing + 1,000 legitimate)
Baseline training corpora (used by baselines only): Enron dataset, TREC Public Spam Corpus, PhishingCorpus
quantitative_results: GPT-4 normal prompt: 99.70% accuracy / 99.70% precision / 99.70% recall (TP=1007, FP=3, TN=997, FN=3)
GPT-4 simple prompt: 99.35% accuracy, 99.60% precision, 99.11% recall (FP=4)
GPT-3.5 normal prompt: 96.92% accuracy, 96.84% precision, 97.03% recall (FP=32, FN=30)
GPT-3.5 simple prompt: 84.13% accuracy, 99.15% precision, 69.01% recall
Llama2-70B normal prompt: 79.05% accuracy, 72.46% precision, 94.06% recall (FP=361)
Llama2-70B simple prompt: 88.61% accuracy, 98.87% precision, 78.22% recall
Gemini Pro normal prompt: 98.01% accuracy, 97.92% precision, 98.12% recall
Gemini Pro simple prompt: 98.06% accuracy, 99.39% precision, 96.73% recall
Prompt-engineering recall gain (normal vs simple): GPT-3.5 +28.02%, Llama2 +15.84%; Llama2 normal-prompt precision dropped 26.41%
Baseline accuracies on the paper's dataset: Baseline A (CNN) 60.00%, Baseline B (TF-IDF+SVM) 57.26%, Baseline C (bag-of-words+NN) 54.53%, Baseline D (header features+random forest) 86.22%
API latency: 12.53s avg for GPT-4, 2.50s for GPT-3.5
Cost for 2,010 emails: GPT-4 normal $265.80 / simple $249.93; GPT-3.5 normal $13.68 / simple $12.62
GPT-4 detected 124 phishing emails that impersonated no brand via SE cues alone; GPT-4 phishing_score TN peaked at 0 (604 cases)
baselines_compared: Llama2-70B (self-hosted on Azure ML, 4x NVIDIA A100)
Baseline A [3]: CNN on tokenized email body (trained on Enron)
Baseline D [10]: header-feature extraction + random forest (trained on TREC + PhishingCorpus)
Also compared 'normal' engineered prompt vs 'simple' one-line prompt as an internal ablation
